historical_tariffs.title: Solar Panel Safeguard Tariffs Begin
historical_tariffs.price_impact: Solar installation costs increased 10-18%
historical_tariffs.details: Initial safeguard tariffs on imported solar panels began raising costs for residential and commercial installations.

historical_tariffs.title: Lumber Dispute with Canada Escalates
historical_tariffs.price_impact: Softwood lumber prices +20%, new home costs +$1,300
historical_tariffs.details: Commerce Department imposed countervailing duties averaging 20% on Canadian softwood lumber, significantly impacting construction costs.

historical_tariffs.title: Steel and Aluminum Section 232 Tariffs
historical_tariffs.price_impact: Cars +$300-500, appliances +$50-150, beer/soda +$0.10-0.15 per can
historical_tariffs.source: Federal Reserve Bank of New York
historical_tariffs.details: 25% tariff on steel, 10% on aluminum. Washing machine prices jumped $86-100. Auto prices rose $300-500. Beer and soda in aluminum cans more expensive.

historical_tariffs.title: Washing Machine Tariffs
historical_tariffs.price_impact: Washing machines +$86-100 (12% increase)
historical_tariffs.details: Targeted tariffs on imported washing machines led to price increases across all brands, even those made domestically, as manufacturers took advantage of reduced competition.

historical_tariffs.title: China Section 301 Tariffs - Wave 1
historical_tariffs.price_impact: Limited direct consumer impact initially
historical_tariffs.details: First wave of tariffs on $34 billion of Chinese goods, primarily industrial products. Consumer impact minimal at this stage.

historical_tariffs.title: China Tariffs Expand - Consumer Goods Hit
historical_tariffs.price_impact: Consumer goods +5-15% depending on category
historical_tariffs.details: Tariffs expanded to $200 billion in Chinese goods, including consumer products. Furniture, luggage, and tools saw notable price increases.

historical_tariffs.title: China Tariffs Raised to 25%
historical_tariffs.price_impact: Average household cost +$600-800 annually
historical_tariffs.details: Tariff rate on $200 billion of Chinese goods increased from 10% to 25%. Major impact on consumer goods across categories.

historical_tariffs.title: Additional China Tariffs on Remaining Goods
historical_tariffs.price_impact: Electronics +3-8%, clothing +8-12%, toys +10-15%
historical_tariffs.details: Tariffs extended to nearly all remaining Chinese imports including consumer electronics, clothing, and toys. Holiday shopping affected.

historical_tariffs.title: COVID-19 Medical Supply Tariffs Suspended
historical_tariffs.price_impact: Tariff relief helped stabilize medical supply prices during pandemic
historical_tariffs.details: Temporary tariff exemptions on critical medical supplies to combat COVID-19 pandemic. Helped prevent additional price spikes on already scarce medical equipment.

historical_tariffs.title: Phase One Trade Deal - Some Tariff Relief
historical_tariffs.price_impact: Minor relief on select products, most tariffs remain
historical_tariffs.details: Phase One trade deal with China provided limited tariff relief on select products but maintained most consumer goods tariffs.

historical_tariffs.title: Lumber Tariffs on Canadian Imports Doubled
historical_tariffs.price_impact: New home prices increased $14,000-$24,000 on average
historical_tariffs.details: Tariffs on Canadian lumber jumped from 9% to 17.9%, contributing to housing affordability crisis. Single-family home construction costs rose significantly.

historical_tariffs.title: Container Shipping Costs Surge
historical_tariffs.price_impact: Shipping costs +300-400%, adding to tariff burden
historical_tariffs.details: While not a tariff, shipping cost explosion compounded tariff impacts. Combined effect raised prices significantly across all imported goods.

historical_tariffs.title: Steel and Aluminum Tariffs Maintained
historical_tariffs.price_impact: Washing machines +$86, cars +$200-$400, appliances +12-15%
historical_tariffs.details: Continuation of Section 232 tariffs (25% steel, 10% aluminum). Manufacturers passed costs to consumers across multiple product categories.

historical_tariffs.title: China Section 301 Tariffs Extended
historical_tariffs.price_impact: Consumer goods 7.5-25% tariffs, added $625-$831 annual cost per household
historical_tariffs.details: Tariffs on $370 billion in Chinese goods maintained. Affected everyday items from phones to furniture, with costs largely passed to American consumers.

historical_tariffs.title: Russian Import Ban (Sanctions)
historical_tariffs.price_impact: Gas prices +$0.50-1.00 per gallon, seafood +15-25%
historical_tariffs.details: Ban on Russian imports (not technically tariffs but import restrictions) significantly impacted energy and food prices.

historical_tariffs.title: Solar Panel Tariff Relief
historical_tariffs.price_impact: Solar installation costs decreased 8-12%
historical_tariffs.details: Two-year pause on solar panel tariffs to accelerate clean energy adoption. Made residential solar more affordable for homeowners.

historical_tariffs.title: Avocado and Tomato Pricing Pressures
historical_tariffs.price_impact: Produce prices 15-30% above previous year
historical_tariffs.details: Combination of seasonal tariffs, weather disruptions, and supply chain issues drove fresh produce costs higher, particularly Mexican imports.

historical_tariffs.title: Lumber Tariff Rates Fluctuate
historical_tariffs.price_impact: Rates dropped to 8% providing some relief
historical_tariffs.details: Canadian lumber tariff rates reduced from 17.9% to 8%, providing modest relief for construction costs.

historical_tariffs.title: Electric Vehicle Battery Component Tariffs
historical_tariffs.price_impact: EVs increased $2,000-$4,500 depending on model
historical_tariffs.details: New tariffs on battery components from China aimed at boosting domestic production. Short-term effect raised EV prices, potentially slowing adoption.

historical_tariffs.title: Semiconductor Tariff Exemptions
historical_tariffs.price_impact: Prevented estimated 5-8% price increases on electronics
historical_tariffs.details: Exemptions on critical semiconductor imports helped stabilize consumer electronics pricing during chip shortage recovery.

historical_tariffs.title: Coffee Import Tariff Adjustments
historical_tariffs.price_impact: Specialty coffee +$1.50-$3 per pound at retail
historical_tariffs.details: Tariff changes on coffee imports from select countries. Combined with climate-related supply issues, pushed premium coffee prices higher.

historical_tariffs.title: Pharmaceutical Ingredient Tariffs
historical_tariffs.price_impact: Generic medications increased 6-10%
historical_tariffs.details: Tariffs on active pharmaceutical ingredients raised production costs for generic drug manufacturers, leading to price increases.

historical_tariffs.title: Apparel and Footwear Tariff Increases
historical_tariffs.price_impact: Clothing +8-15%, footwear +12-20%
historical_tariffs.details: Expanded tariffs on textile imports particularly impacted budget-conscious shoppers, with lower-priced items seeing largest percentage increases.

historical_tariffs.title: Aluminum Can Shortage Tariff Impact
historical_tariffs.price_impact: 12-packs increased $0.75-$1.50, canned goods +5-8%
historical_tariffs.details: Aluminum tariffs combined with supply constraints drove up packaging costs, affecting everything from soda to canned vegetables.

historical_tariffs.title: Supreme Court Strikes Down IEEPA Tariffs
historical_tariffs.price_impact: Tariff rate dropped from 16.9% to 9.1% - potential household savings of $700-1,000 annually
historical_tariffs.source: Yale Budget Lab, Supreme Court ruling
historical_tariffs.details: Supreme Court ruled 6-3 that President Trump's use of IEEPA (International Emergency Economic Powers Act) to impose sweeping tariffs exceeded presidential authority. Eliminated reciprocal, fentanyl, and universal baseline tariffs affecting ~$180 billion in annual tariff revenue. Largest single tariff rollback in modern history.

historical_tariffs.title: Section 122 Emergency Tariffs Imposed
historical_tariffs.price_impact: 15% universal tariff raised effective rate back to 13.7%, adding $500-800 per household
historical_tariffs.details: Two days after SCOTUS ruling, President Trump invoked Section 122 of 1974 Trade Act imposing 15% tariffs on all countries for maximum 150 days. USMCA countries (Canada, Mexico) largely exempted. Uncertainty remains whether these will expire or be made permanent.

historical_tariffs.title: Businesses Begin Passing Tariff Costs to Consumers
historical_tariffs.price_impact: Prices expected to rise 4-6% in 2026 as businesses pass along costs
historical_tariffs.details: After absorbing 80% of tariff costs in 2025, businesses began passing costs to consumers in early 2026. Groceries particularly affected due to thin margins. Companies that stockpiled inventory in 2025 now facing full tariff impact.

historical_tariffs.title: Proposed Greenland Tariffs
historical_tariffs.price_impact: Would add 10% tariffs on 8 European countries, increasing household costs $150-200
historical_tariffs.details: President Trump proposed 10% additional tariffs on Denmark, Norway, Sweden, France, Germany, UK, Netherlands, Finland as part of Greenland purchase negotiations. Would push effective tariff rate to 17.5%, highest since 1932. Implementation status uncertain.

historical_tariffs.title: Cumulative 2025-2026 Tariff Impact
historical_tariffs.price_impact: Est. $1,200-$1,750 per household annually, inflation +0.5-1.3 percentage points
historical_tariffs.source: Yale Budget Lab, Morningstar, Goldman Sachs
historical_tariffs.details: Total tariff burden from 2025-2026 policies estimated at $1,750 per household (pre-substitution). Tariffs responsible for pushing inflation from 2% to 2.7% by end of 2025. Core goods prices expected to rise 4.5% cumulatively through 2027.

historical_tariffs.title: January 2026 Inflation Report
historical_tariffs.price_impact: Overall inflation 2.4%, but necessities higher: beef +15%, coffee +18%, electricity +6%
historical_tariffs.details: While overall inflation moderated to 2.4% in January, tariff-affected categories showed sharp increases. Furniture and appliances hit particularly hard. Combination of tariff impacts and supply constraints driving prices for specific necessities well above overall inflation rate.

summary.major_2026_events: Supreme Court struck down IEEPA tariffs (largest rollback in modern history), Section 122 emergency tariffs imposed, businesses passing costs to consumers
summary.current_status: Effective tariff rate 13.7% as of March 2026, legal uncertainty over Section 122 expiration
